exemplar.title: The Effect of Temperature on Particle Wall Collision Frequency in an Ideal Gas

detailedExemplarData.title: The Effect of Temperature on Particle Wall Collision Frequency in an Ideal Gas

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data processing is carried out appropriately and accurately, with averages, ratios and linear regressions (RÂ²) correctly calculated.
Communication of data recording and processing is clear: raw and processed tables have headings, units, uncertainties, and informative graphs.
Insightful normalization of collision counts and inclusion of error bars aid interpretation.
weaknesses: Minor labelling errors (duplicated table numbers, missing units in one header) reduce precision.
Uncertainty propagation into regression parameters is not performed.
Graph equations are not reported numerically, and some layout inconsistencies remain.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data Analysis shows clear and precise communication of processing steps and appropriate, accurate calculations. The omission of full uncertainty propagation and minor labelling/layout issues prevent the top band. A score of 4 reflects strong, reproducible analysis with some significant omissions.

strengths: Research question framed within a specific and appropriate context (ideal gas, PHET simulation, defined temperature range).
Comprehensive background theory (idealâgas law, MaxwellâBoltzmann and collisionâfrequency equations) grounding the investigation.
Detailed listing of variables with units, labelled screenshot, and clear stepâbyâstep procedure.
weaknesses: Procedure omits container dimensions and timeâstep details, introducing reproducibility ambiguities.
Minor contradictions in particle injection instructions (250 vs. 500).
Narrative style and lack of software version/date reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research Design meets the topâband descriptor with a wellâcontextualized question and clear theoretical grounding. The methodological description largely allows reproduction, though minor ambiguities (container size, timeâstep, injection contradiction) and narrative informality prevent a perfect 6. A holistic score of 5 reflects strong performance with room for tightening precision.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly states the observed linear increase in collision frequency with temperature.
Use of high RÂ² values to support the conclusion demonstrates consistency with the analysis.
weaknesses: No quantitative comparison of measured gradient with the theoretical âT dependence.
Superficial linkage to accepted scientific context; theoretical formula is not integrated into the final discussion.
Uncertaintyâs impact on the conclusion is not assessed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified by and consistent with the data, meriting the upper end of band 3â4 for relevance and support. However, a lack of theoretical comparison and only superficial context comparison places the work in band 1â2 for scientific context. Balancing these, a holistic score of 3 captures this mixed performance.

strengths: Identifies random variability as a methodological limitation.
Suggests realistic improvements (additional trials, realâlab experiment).
weaknesses: Evaluation of weaknesses remains genericâno specific systematic errors (timeâstep, sampling inconsistencies) are analysed.
Suggested improvements are not clearly linked to identified limitations or their impacts.
No explanation of how proposed improvements would address the limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation acknowledges genuine limitations and proposes realistic improvements, fitting the top of band 1â2. However, the lack of detailed analysis of specific methodological weaknesses and no explanation of the impact of improvements limits the score to 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames the research question within a clear and specific context: the kinetic-theory description of an ideal gas, limited to a temperature range of 250â500 K and a fixed particle number inside a PHET simulation.  Background theory (ideal-gas assumptions, Maxwell-Boltzmann speed expression and collision-frequency equation) is supplied so the reader understands why temperature should influence wall-collision rate.  Because the context is tightly linked to the chosen variables, apparatus (simulation) and theoretical model, the descriptor for the top band is met, though minor informality and narrative elements reduce precision slightly, justifying 5 rather than 6.

The student calculates absolute and percentage uncertainties for every temperature and shows error bars; one worked example is provided.  Nevertheless, only random error is treatedâno discussion of simulation resolution or timing accuracy appears, and propagation of uncertainty into gradients or the final conclusion is absent.  These omissions align with "some significant omissions", so 3 is awarded.

Key variables (temperature, number of particles, container size, particle mass and sample time) are identified and most are justified.  Random uncertainty is addressed through multiple trials and an absolute/percentage-uncertainty calculation.  However, the student does not discuss systematic issues such as finite time-step accuracy of the simulation or possible bias from particle injection, so the considerations are descriptive rather than fully explained.  This satisfies the band 3-4 descriptor, deserving 4.

The evaluation mentions "random error of the simulation" and asserts that human error is absent, but it does not identify specific weaknesses such as inconsistent sampling interval, limited temperature range, or lack of systematic-error treatment.  These comments are generic rather than detailed, so the work fits the 1-2 band; a mark of 2 is given because at least one genuine limitation (random variability) is acknowledged.

The procedure lists step-by-step actions (setting particle count, temperature increments, eight repeats, 5 ps counting interval) and supplies a link plus a labelled screenshot.  A competent reader could reproduce the investigation with only a few questions: the container size is not stated, step 3 contradicts the earlier instruction when 250 particles are used, and the sampling period in the screenshot shows 10 ps not 5 ps.  These small ambiguities fit the descriptor of a method that is reproducible "with few ambiguities", placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band; the level of detail merits the upper mark of 4.

Averages, ratios, uncertainties and linear regressions (with RÂ² values) are calculated correctly, and the processed data are consistent with the raw values.  Minor inconsistencies such as a mislabeled ratio column and the unverified equation lines on the graphs limit perfection, but no major inaccuracies are evident.  Hence processing is judged "appropriate and accurate", meriting a 4.

Suggested improvements are limited to the idea of repeating trials and, very generally, performing a real-lab experiment.  They are realistic but not linked to the specific weaknesses identified, nor is their impact explained.  This satisfies the descriptor "realistic improvements are stated" in band 1-2, so 2 marks are awarded.

Raw and processed data tables are present for both particle numbers; headings, units and uncertainties are mostly supplied, and graphs include axes labels, titles and error bars.  Layout issues (duplicated table numbers, missing unit in one column) reduce precision, yet overall the communication is clear enough for readers to follow processing unambiguously.  This matches the descriptor "either clear or precise" and approaches both, so a 4 is appropriate.

Although kinetic-theory formulae are presented in the background section, the student never compares the measured gradients or ratios with the theoretical expression z_W = Â¼ n vââT.  The discussion merely notes the existence of a linear relationship and speculates qualitatively about particle crowding.  This is a superficial linkage to accepted science, corresponding to band 1-2; the brief reference to theory earns the top mark within that band, 2.

The student states that increasing temperature increases collision frequency and supports this with high RÂ² values from the regression analysis for both particle counts.  The conclusion is consistent with the plotted trends and acknowledges random errors.  However, it does not quantify how uncertainty might influence acceptance of linearity, nor does it connect to a theoretical gradient, so the justification is good but not complete, fitting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The question uses âTo what extentâ but remains qualitative. Consider specifying the expected functional relationship (e.g. collision frequency ââT) to sharpen the research aim.

The research question is framed within a specific context (ideal gas, PHET simulation, temperature range), effectively situating the investigation. This provides strong clarity on scope and variables.
Background clearly outlines ideal-gas assumptions, demonstrating solid grounding in theory before experimentation.
The PV = nRT equation is presented well, but its direct connection to collision frequency could be highlighted to bridge theory and measured variable.
The hypothesis states a general relationship but could be strengthened by specifying the expected mathematical form, e.g. collision frequency ââT.
The simulation description omits container dimensions and time-step details, which are crucial for reproducibility and evaluating systematic uncertainties.
The question omits container dimensions. Including the volume or side length would remove ambiguity and improve reproducibility.
The introduction is engaging and sets a narrative, but its informality could be tightened. A concise statement of purpose would strengthen clarity.
The calculation of absolute and percentage uncertainties is executed correctly and documented with worked examples.
Uncertainty propagation is not carried through to the regression analysis or final gradient; consider propagating errors into slope and intercept.
The formula for percentage uncertainty is applied, but its impact on later results is not assessedâthis omission could obscure significant errors.
While the simulation link is provided, include version or date accessed to ensure the correct software build is identifiable.
Step 3 instructs injecting 500 particles even when testing at 250; this contradiction needs clarification to avoid procedural ambiguity.
The labelled screenshot of the simulation setup is clear and aids reproducibility.
The narrative on selecting 500 and 250 particles explains rationale, but lacks quantitative justification relating to statistical reliability.
Independent and dependent variables are explicitly defined with units and increments, facilitating clarity in data collection.
Control variables description omits simulationâs time-step stability and algorithmic biases; these systematic sources of error should be discussed.
Explicitly justify the chosen temperature increments (50âK) with reference to the expected variation in collision frequency.
Raw data tables comprehensively list eight repeats per temperature, demonstrating robust data collection.
Table headers lack units for the collision counts (runs), reducing precision in communication of recorded data.
Duplicate table numbering (âTable 1.1â for both particle counts) can confuse readers; ensure unique labels.
Processed tables present averages, ratios and uncertainties neatly, facilitating transparent data interpretation.
The 500-particle processed table has empty headers above â500 Particles,â which hinders immediate comprehension.
Normalizing collision counts by particle number (ratio) is insightful, enabling direct comparison across datasets.
Graphs include clear regression lines with RÂ² values, strongly supporting the linear relationship claim.
The conclusion does not compare the measured gradient to the theoretical z_W=Â¼nâ(8kT/Ïm) expression. Integrate theory to deepen justification.
Asserting âno human errorâ is generic; instead evaluate digital measurement precision and any algorithmic assumptions.
Works cited list is comprehensive, drawing from diverse and reputable sources relevant to collision theory and simulation.
Include numerical values for slope and intercept of regression lines to quantify the relationship more rigorously.
Error bars are plotted but their statistical significance is not discussed; consider adding notes on confidence intervals or standard errors in captions.
The conclusion succinctly states the linear correlation with high RÂ² values, directly reflecting the quantitative analysis.
The speculation on particle crowding affecting ratios is creative; suggest designing a follow-up simulation to test this hypothesis.
Evaluation acknowledges random error reduction but omits discussion of simulation resolution (time-step) limitations.
Consider systematic-error testing by varying the simulationâs time-step and comparing collision counts to gauge algorithmic bias.
Citation style is inconsistent (varying italics, punctuation, URL formatting). Standardize to a single format (e.g. APA) for consistency.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
